name: Hip Circle
description: A dynamic circular movement that lubricates the hip joint, improves range of motion and reduces stiffness.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, hands on hips.
Slowly rotate your hips in a large clockwise circle.
Complete 10 circles clockwise, then 10 counter-clockwise.
Keep your upper body still — only your hips should move.
Gradually increase the size of each circle as you warm up.

name: World's Greatest Stretch
description: A compound mobility drill targeting hips, thoracic spine, and hamstrings — one of the best pre-workout movements.
instructions: Start in a high plank position.
Step your right foot forward outside your right hand.
Drop your left knee to the floor.
Rotate your right arm up toward the ceiling, following with your eyes.
Hold 2 seconds, return to plank, repeat on left side.
Perform 5 reps per side.

name: Ankle Circles
description: Improves ankle mobility and flexibility, reducing risk of ankle sprains and improving balance.
instructions: Sit or stand on one leg, lifting the other foot off the ground.
Slowly rotate the raised foot clockwise 10 times.
Then rotate counter-clockwise 10 times.
Try to make the circles as large as possible.
Switch legs and repeat.

name: Thoracic Rotation
description: Opens up the mid-back and improves rotational mobility — essential for athletes and desk workers alike.
instructions: Kneel on all fours, spine neutral.
Place one hand behind your head, elbow pointing out.
Slowly rotate that elbow toward the opposite arm on the floor.
Then open it up toward the ceiling as far as comfortable.
Perform 10 reps on each side, moving slowly and with control.

name: Cat-Cow Stretch
description: A flowing spinal movement that improves flexibility, relieves tension and coordinates breath with movement.
instructions: Start on hands and knees, wrists under shoulders, knees under hips.
Inhale: drop your belly toward the floor, lift your head and tailbone (Cow).
Exhale: round your spine toward the ceiling, tuck chin and tailbone (Cat).
Move slowly and breathe deeply with each transition.
Perform 10–15 cycles.

name: Shoulder CARs
description: Controlled Articular Rotations (CARs) for the shoulder — maintains full joint health and range of motion.
instructions: Stand tall, feet shoulder-width apart.
Slowly raise one arm forward, up overhead, then behind you in a big circle.
Move as slowly as possible — the slower the better.
Keep the rest of your body completely still.
Perform 3–5 circles each direction, each arm.

name: 90/90 Hip Stretch
description: The 90/90 position works both internal and external hip rotation simultaneously — excellent for tight hips.
instructions: Sit on the floor with both legs bent at 90 degrees — one in front, one to the side.
Keep your back tall and sit upright.
Lean forward over your front shin gently.
Hold 30–60 seconds, then switch sides.
Perform 2–3 sets per side.

name: Wrist Circles
description: Improves wrist mobility and prevents injury — essential before any pushing or pressing exercise.
instructions: Extend both arms in front of you, hands open.
Make fists and rotate your wrists clockwise 10 times.
Then counter-clockwise 10 times.
Open and close your hands 10 times to finish.
Perform daily before training or computer work.

name: Inchworm Walk
description: A dynamic stretch that simultaneously opens hamstrings, calves, shoulders and warms up the whole body.
instructions: Stand tall, feet hip-width apart.
Bend forward and place hands on the floor in front of your feet.
Walk hands forward until you are in a plank position.
Hold 1 second, then walk feet toward hands.
Stand up and repeat for 5–8 reps, travelling forward.

name: Deep Squat Hold
description: Improves hip, ankle and thoracic mobility simultaneously. A fundamental human movement pattern.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out.
Slowly lower into a deep squat, keeping heels on the floor.
Bring your elbows inside your knees and press them out gently.
Hold for 30–60 seconds, breathing deeply.
Use a door frame or TRX for support if needed.

name: Standing Hamstring Stretch
description: The most fundamental stretch for the posterior chain — relieves lower back tension and improves flexibility.
instructions: Stand tall with feet together.
Slowly bend forward from the hips, not the waist.
Let your hands reach toward the floor — don't bounce.
Hold the end position for 30–45 seconds.
Slowly roll back up. Perform 3 times.

name: Standing Quad Stretch
description: Essential after any leg workout — reduces DOMS and improves knee health.
instructions: Stand on one leg, use a wall for balance if needed.
Bend the opposite knee and grab your ankle behind you.
Gently pull your heel toward your glutes.
Keep your knees together and stand tall.
Hold 30–45 seconds each leg.

name: Hip Flexor Lunge Stretch
description: Counteracts hours of sitting — opens the hip flexors and reduces lower back pain.
instructions: Kneel on your right knee, left foot forward at 90 degrees.
Shift your hips forward gently until you feel a stretch in the right hip.
Keep your back straight and core engaged.
Raise your right arm overhead to deepen the stretch.
Hold 30–45 seconds, switch sides. Perform 2–3 sets.

name: Cobra Stretch
description: Opens the chest and abdomen, decompresses the lower spine. A yoga classic for all fitness levels.
instructions: Lie face down, palms flat on the floor near your shoulders.
Press through your hands to lift your chest off the floor.
Keep elbows slightly bent and shoulders away from ears.
Look slightly upward — don't crank your neck.
Hold 20–30 seconds. Repeat 3 times.

name: Child's Pose
description: A restorative yoga pose that releases the lower back, hips and shoulders — perfect post-workout cool down.
instructions: Kneel on the floor, big toes touching, knees wide apart.
Sit back on your heels and fold forward, arms extended on the floor.
Rest your forehead on the mat.
Breathe deeply and let gravity deepen the stretch.
Hold 60–90 seconds. Perfect as a recovery pose.

name: Pigeon Pose
description: The ultimate hip opener — deeply stretches the piriformis and glutes, relieving sciatic pain.
instructions: From downward dog, bring your right knee forward behind your right wrist.
Extend your left leg back on the floor.
Square your hips toward the floor.
Fold forward over your front shin for a deeper stretch.
Hold 60–90 seconds each side.

name: Doorway Chest Stretch
description: Opens the chest and anterior shoulder — essential for anyone who sits at a desk or trains chest frequently.
instructions: Stand in a doorway, arms at 90 degrees like goalposts.
Place forearms on the door frame.
Step one foot forward and lean gently through the doorway.
Feel the stretch across your chest and front shoulders.
Hold 30–45 seconds. Perform 3 times.

name: Seated Spinal Twist
description: Improves spinal rotation and releases tension in the mid-back — great after long periods of sitting.
instructions: Sit tall on the floor, legs extended.
Bend your right knee and cross it over your left leg.
Place your right hand on the floor behind you.
Wrap your left arm around your right knee and twist right.
Hold 30 seconds, switch sides. Breathe deeply throughout.

name: Calf Stretch on Wall
description: Prevents Achilles injuries and relieves calf tightness — vital for runners and leg day recovery.
instructions: Stand facing a wall, hands on the wall at shoulder height.
Step one foot back, heel flat on the floor.
Lean forward to feel the stretch in the back calf.
Keep the back knee straight for gastrocnemius, or slightly bent for soleus.
Hold 30–45 seconds each leg.

name: Butterfly Stretch
description: Opens the inner thighs and groin — reduces stiffness from running, cycling or sitting.
instructions: Sit on the floor, bring the soles of your feet together in front of you.
Hold your feet with both hands.
Gently press your knees toward the floor using your elbows.
Keep your back straight — don't hunch.
Hold 45–60 seconds. Perform 2–3 times.

name: Pelvic Tilt
description: Strengthens the deep core and pelvic floor — essential preparation for pregnancy and childbirth.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent, feet flat on the floor.
Take a deep breath in.
As you exhale, gently flatten your lower back against the floor by tightening your abs.
Hold for 5 seconds, then release.
Perform 10–15 reps, 2–3 sets daily.

name: Bird-Dog
description: Builds spinal stability and core strength without compression — safe and effective pre-pregnancy.
instructions: Start on all fours, wrists under shoulders, knees under hips.
Extend your right arm forward and left leg back simultaneously.
Keep your hips level and spine neutral — don't rotate.
Hold 3 seconds, then slowly return.
Alternate sides for 10 reps each. Perform 3 sets.

name: Glute Bridge
description: Strengthens glutes and pelvic floor — helps prepare the body for the demands of pregnancy.
instructions: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat, hip-width apart.
Press through your heels and lift your hips toward the ceiling.
Squeeze your glutes at the top — hold 2 seconds.
Slowly lower back down.
Perform 15 reps, 3 sets. Add a resistance band for progression.

name: Clamshell Exercise
description: Targets the glute medius — essential for pelvic stability and reducing hip pain during pregnancy.
instructions: Lie on your side with hips stacked, knees bent at 45 degrees.
Keep your feet together.
Slowly raise your top knee as high as possible without rotating your hips.
Hold 1–2 seconds at the top, then slowly lower.
Perform 15 reps each side, 3 sets.

name: Wall Squat
description: Safe squat variation that builds leg strength while supporting the lower back — perfect pre-pregnancy.
instructions: Stand with your back flat against a wall.
Walk your feet out about 2 feet from the wall.
Slide down the wall until your knees are at 90 degrees.
Hold for 20–30 seconds, breathing steadily.
Perform 3–5 holds. Increase time as you get stronger.

name: Standing Cat-Cow
description: A standing version of cat-cow that reduces back pain and improves spinal mobility safely.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, hands on thighs.
Slightly bend your knees.
Inhale: arch your lower back, lift your chest (cow position).
Exhale: round your back, tuck your chin and pelvis (cat position).
Perform 10–15 slow cycles, coordinating with breath.

name: Kegel Exercise
description: The most important exercise for pregnancy preparation — strengthens the pelvic floor muscles for labour and recovery.
instructions: Sit, stand, or lie down comfortably.
Identify the pelvic floor muscles — imagine you're stopping the flow of urine.
Contract those muscles for 5 seconds.
Relax completely for 5 seconds.
Perform 10–15 reps, 3 times a day. Gradually increase hold time to 10 seconds.

name: Side-Lying Hip Abduction
description: Strengthens outer hips and glutes — reduces hip pain and prepares the pelvis for pregnancy.
instructions: Lie on your side, legs straight, body in a straight line.
Keep your top foot flexed.
Slowly raise your top leg to about 45 degrees.
Hold 1 second at the top, then slowly lower.
Perform 15 reps each side, 3 sets.

name: Seated Hip Circles
description: Gently mobilizes the hip joint and relieves pelvic tension — safe for all stages of pre-pregnancy.
instructions: Sit on a stability ball or chair.
Slowly rotate your hips in a large clockwise circle.
Keep your upper body upright.
Perform 10 circles clockwise, then 10 counter-clockwise.
Breathe deeply throughout.

name: Resistance Band Row
description: Strengthens the upper back and posture muscles — counteracts the forward pull of pregnancy.
instructions: Sit tall in a chair or on the floor, band looped around your feet.
Hold one end in each hand, arms extended.
Pull the band toward your belly button, elbows going back.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end.
Slowly release. Perform 15 reps, 3 sets.

name: Diaphragmatic Breathing
description: The very first exercise after delivery — reconnects the deep core and pelvic floor through breath.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent.
Place one hand on your chest, one on your belly.
Breathe in slowly through your nose — let your belly rise, not your chest.
As you exhale, gently draw your belly button toward your spine.
Perform 10 deep breaths, 3 times daily from day 1 postpartum.

name: Heel Slides
description: Gently reactivates the deep core without strain — safe to begin within days of delivery.
instructions: Lie on your back, knees bent.
Take a breath in, then exhale and gently activate your deep core.
Slowly slide one heel along the floor, extending the leg.
Return to start without losing your core connection.
Alternate legs for 10 reps each, 2 sets.

name: Postpartum Glute Bridge
description: Safely rebuilds glute and pelvic floor strength post-delivery — a foundational recovery exercise.
instructions: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat, hip-width apart.
Inhale to prepare.
Exhale, engage your pelvic floor and lift your hips slowly.
Hold at the top for 3 seconds, squeezing glutes gently.
Lower slowly. Perform 10 reps, 2–3 sets.

name: Dead Bug
description: Rebuilds deep core coordination safely — one of the best postpartum exercises for abdominal recovery.
instructions: Lie on your back, arms pointing toward ceiling, knees at 90 degrees.
Flatten your lower back against the floor.
Slowly lower your right arm and left leg toward the floor simultaneously.
Return to start without arching your back.
Alternate sides for 8 reps each, 2 sets.

name: Modified Wall Push-Up
description: Rebuilds upper body strength safely after delivery — avoids abdominal pressure of floor push-ups.
instructions: Stand an arm's length from a wall, hands on wall at shoulder height.
Inhale as you slowly lower your chest toward the wall.
Exhale and engage core as you push back to start.
Keep your body in a straight line — no sagging hips.
Perform 10–15 reps, 2–3 sets.

name: Postpartum Clamshell
description: Rebuilds hip stability and glute medius strength lost during pregnancy — reduces pelvic pain.
instructions: Lie on your side, hips stacked, knees bent at 45 degrees.
Keep feet together and hips still.
Raise your top knee as high as possible without rotating.
Lower slowly with control.
Perform 15 reps each side, 3 sets.

name: Seated Posture Row
description: Counteracts the hunching from breastfeeding — rebuilds upper back strength and posture.
instructions: Sit tall, band looped around a stable anchor or your feet.
Hold the band with arms extended.
Pull toward your belly, squeezing shoulder blades together.
Hold 2 seconds, release slowly.
Perform 15 reps, 3 sets.

name: Postpartum Bird-Dog
description: Restores spinal stability and coordination after pregnancy — safe from 6 weeks postpartum.
instructions: On all fours, spine neutral.
Slowly extend right arm and left leg simultaneously.
Hold 3 seconds without rotating your hips or spine.
Return with control, then switch sides.
Perform 8 reps per side, 2 sets.

name: Seated Pelvic Floor Lift
description: Rebuilds pelvic floor strength post-delivery — essential for bladder control and core recovery.
instructions: Sit comfortably on a chair, feet flat on floor.
Breathe in deeply.
As you exhale, lift and squeeze your pelvic floor muscles.
Hold the lift for 5–10 seconds while continuing to breathe.
Release completely. Perform 10 reps, 3 times daily.

name: Supported Side-Lying Leg Lift
description: Gently rebuilds hip strength and stability while protecting the healing core and pelvic floor.
instructions: Lie on your side, head resting on your lower arm.
Keep your body in a straight line.
Slowly raise your top leg to about 30–40 degrees.
Hold 2 seconds, lower with control.
Perform 12 reps each side, 2 sets.

name: Box Breathing
description: Used by Navy SEALs and elite athletes — resets the nervous system, reduces stress and improves focus.
instructions: Sit upright, close your eyes.
Exhale all air from your lungs.
Inhale through your nose for 4 counts.
Hold your breath for 4 counts.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for 4 counts.
Hold empty for 4 counts. This is 1 cycle. Perform 4–6 cycles.

name: 4-7-8 Breathing
description: Dr. Andrew Weil's technique — activates the parasympathetic nervous system, reduces anxiety and aids sleep.
instructions: Sit comfortably or lie down.
Exhale completely through your mouth.
Inhale through your nose for 4 counts.
Hold your breath for 7 counts.
Exhale completely through your mouth for 8 counts.
Repeat 3–4 times. Perform before bed or when stressed.

name: Diaphragmatic Belly Breathing
description: Teaches correct breathing mechanics — most people breathe shallowly from the chest. This fixes it.
instructions: Lie on your back, one hand on chest, one on belly.
Breathe in slowly through your nose for 4 counts.
Your belly should rise — your chest should barely move.
Exhale slowly for 6 counts — belly falls.
Perform 10 breaths. Practice daily for 5 minutes.

name: Alternate Nostril Breathing
description: An ancient yogic breathing technique that balances the two hemispheres of the brain and calms the mind.
instructions: Sit comfortably, spine tall.
Close your right nostril with your right thumb.
Inhale through the left nostril for 4 counts.
Close both nostrils, hold for 4 counts.
Open the right nostril, exhale for 4 counts.
Inhale through right, switch sides. Perform 5–10 cycles.

name: Pursed Lip Breathing
description: Slows breathing rate, improves oxygen exchange and relieves shortness of breath — great post-cardio.
instructions: Relax your neck and shoulders.
Inhale slowly through your nose for 2 counts.
Pucker your lips as if you are about to whistle.
Exhale slowly through pursed lips for 4 counts.
Practice for 5–10 minutes. Use anytime you feel breathless.

name: Lion's Breath
description: A powerful yoga breathing technique that releases tension in the face, jaw and chest immediately.
instructions: Sit in a comfortable position, hands on knees.
Inhale deeply through your nose.
Open your mouth wide, extend your tongue fully downward.
Exhale forcefully making a 'haaa' sound.
Eyes wide open, gaze upward. Repeat 3–5 times.

name: Ocean Breath (Ujjayi)
description: Creates a soothing ocean-like sound that anchors attention — widely used in yoga and meditation.
instructions: Breathe in through your nose normally.
As you exhale, slightly constrict the back of your throat — like fogging up a mirror.
This creates a soft 'hhhhh' sound.
Inhale with the same slight constriction.
Continue for 5–10 minutes during yoga or meditation.

name: Kapalabhati Breath
description: A vigorous breathing technique that energizes the body, clears the lungs and strengthens the core.
instructions: Sit tall with spine erect.
Inhale deeply through your nose.
Perform short, sharp, forceful exhales through your nose.
Each exhale is driven by a quick contraction of the belly.
Start with 20 pumps, rest, then repeat 3 rounds.

name: Extended Exhale Breathing
description: Doubles the exhale length to activate the parasympathetic system — the fastest way to calm down.
instructions: Sit or lie comfortably.
Inhale through your nose for 4 counts.
Exhale through your mouth for 8 counts — twice as long.
The slow exhale activates the calming part of your nervous system.
Perform 10 cycles. Use before sleep or after intense training.

name: Coherent Breathing
description: 5 breaths per minute breathing that maximises heart rate variability — the gold standard for recovery.
instructions: Sit tall, eyes closed.
Inhale slowly through your nose for 6 counts.
Exhale slowly through your nose for 6 counts.
This gives exactly 5 breaths per minute.
Continue for 10–20 minutes for maximum recovery benefit.

name: Sitali Cooling Breath
description: An ancient yoga cooling breath that lowers body temperature, reduces anger and calms the mind — perfect after hot workouts.
instructions: Sit comfortably with spine tall and eyes closed.
Roll your tongue into a tube shape (or purse lips if unable).
Inhale slowly through the rolled tongue — feel the cool air.
Close your mouth and exhale slowly through your nose.
Repeat 8–10 times. Use after exercise or when feeling overheated.

name: Humming Bee Breath (Bhramari)
description: The vibration from humming activates the vagus nerve instantly — one of the fastest ways to calm anxiety.
instructions: Sit comfortably, close your eyes.
Place your index fingers gently over your ear cartilage.
Inhale deeply through your nose.
As you exhale, make a steady humming sound like a bee.
Feel the vibration in your head and face.
Repeat 6–8 times. Excellent before sleep or meditation.

name: Nadi Shodhana (Channel Cleansing)
description: Advanced alternate nostril breathing that purifies energy channels and balances left-right brain hemispheres.
instructions: Sit tall, right hand in Vishnu mudra (fold index and middle fingers).
Close right nostril with thumb, inhale left for 4 counts.
Close both nostrils, hold for 16 counts.
Open right nostril only, exhale for 8 counts.
Inhale right for 4, hold both for 16, exhale left for 8.
This is one round. Perform 3–5 rounds daily.

name: Three-Part Yogic Breath
description: Fills all three chambers of the lungs — belly, ribcage, chest — maximising oxygen intake with every breath.
instructions: Lie down or sit comfortably.
First, breathe into your belly — feel it expand.
Continue inhaling into your ribcage — feel it widen sideways.
Finally fill your upper chest — feel your collarbones rise.
Exhale in reverse: chest, ribs, belly deflate.
Perform 5–8 complete breaths slowly.

name: Wim Hof Power Breathing
description: The famous Wim Hof method — 30 deep power breaths followed by a breath hold that boosts energy and immune function.
instructions: Lie down comfortably. Take 30 deep, full inhales and relaxed exhales.
Each inhale fully fills your lungs — exhale is passive, not forced.
After 30 breaths, exhale fully and hold — no air in lungs.
Hold as long as comfortable (typically 1–3 minutes).
Take one deep recovery breath, hold for 15 seconds.
Repeat 3 rounds. Never do near water or while driving.

name: 5-5-5 Triangle Breathing
description: Equal inhale, hold and exhale creates perfect balance — simpler than box breathing, equally effective for anxiety.
instructions: Sit or lie comfortably, close your eyes.
Inhale slowly through your nose for 5 counts.
Hold your breath gently for 5 counts.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for 5 counts.
This is one triangle. Perform 5–10 cycles.
Use before exams, presentations, or stressful situations.

name: Breath of Fire
description: A rapid rhythmic breath from Kundalini yoga that strengthens the core, builds heat and boosts metabolism.
instructions: Sit tall in cross-legged position.
Begin rapid, equal inhales and exhales through the nose.
Each breath is driven by the navel — pump it in and out.
Start slow (1 breath/second), build to 2–3 per second.
Continue for 1–3 minutes.
Stop if dizzy — rest and breathe normally.

name: Morning Energising Breath
description: A simple 3-step morning breathing sequence that wakes the body, clears the mind and sets a positive tone for the day.
instructions: Stand tall or sit upright immediately after waking.
Take 5 slow, deep belly breaths — eyes closed.
Then 10 short sharp inhales through the nose (sniff-sniff pattern).
Follow with 3 big sighing exhales — 'haaaa' through the mouth.
Raise your arms overhead as you inhale, lower as you exhale.
Repeat the full sequence 3 times. Energises better than coffee!

name: Pre-Workout Power Breath
description: Activates the nervous system and increases oxygen levels before training — used by elite athletes for performance.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Take 3 deep belly breaths to centre yourself.
Then 10 forceful exhales — push air out hard through pursed lips.
Take one massive inhale, hold for 5 seconds, then release.
Repeat 2–3 times.
Start your workout within 60 seconds for maximum benefit.

name: Post-Workout Recovery Breath
description: Lowers heart rate, reduces cortisol and accelerates recovery — the most important 5 minutes after any workout.
instructions: Lie on your back immediately after training.
Place one hand on chest, one on belly.
Breathe in through nose for 4 counts — belly rises.
Hold 2 counts.
Exhale through mouth for 6 counts — belly falls.
Perform 15–20 cycles. Heart rate should drop noticeably.

name: Lizard Pose
description: A deep hip opener that targets the hip flexors, groin and inner thighs — excellent for desk workers and runners.
instructions: From a high plank, step your right foot outside your right hand.
Lower your left knee to the floor.
Walk your right foot slightly to the right for more space.
Lower your forearms to the floor if possible.
Hold 30–60 seconds each side. Breathe deeply.

name: Thread the Needle
description: Releases upper back and shoulder tension — excellent for desk workers and overhead athletes.
instructions: Start on all fours, spine neutral.
Slide your right arm under your left arm along the floor.
Rest your right shoulder and cheek on the floor.
Hold 20–30 seconds, feeling the stretch in upper back.
Return and repeat other side. Perform 3 times each.

name: Standing Figure-4 Stretch
description: Standing piriformis stretch that improves hip mobility and relieves sciatic tension — can be done anywhere.
instructions: Stand near a wall for balance.
Cross your right ankle over your left knee in a figure-4.
Slowly bend your standing knee, lowering into a squat.
Feel the deep stretch in your right glute.
Hold 30 seconds, switch sides.

name: Seated Neck Circles
description: Releases neck tension and improves cervical mobility — essential after screen time or heavy upper body training.
instructions: Sit tall, shoulders relaxed and away from ears.
Slowly drop your right ear toward your right shoulder.
Roll your chin down toward your chest.
Continue to the left shoulder, then look up gently.
Perform 5 slow circles each direction. Never force the movement.

name: Half-Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch
description: The gold standard hip flexor stretch — directly targets the psoas and iliacus shortened by sitting.
instructions: Kneel on your right knee, left foot forward.
Tuck your pelvis under — squeeze right glute.
Shift hips forward until you feel stretch in right hip front.
Raise right arm overhead for a deeper stretch.
Hold 45–60 seconds each side. Perform daily.

name: Doorway Shoulder Stretch
description: Releases tight anterior shoulders from bench pressing, desk work and phone use.
instructions: Stand in a doorway, arm at 90 degrees on the frame.
Step forward until you feel a stretch in chest and shoulder.
Hold 30 seconds. Move arm higher or lower to target different fibres.
Perform both sides. Repeat 3 times each.

name: Lying IT Band Stretch
description: Releases the iliotibial band — one of the most commonly tight structures in runners and cyclists.
instructions: Lie on your back, arms out to the sides.
Bring your right knee to your chest.
Cross it over your body toward the left side.
Keep both shoulders flat on the floor.
Hold 45 seconds each side.

name: Standing Side Stretch
description: Lengthens the lateral chain — obliques, lats and intercostals — reducing side waist tightness.
instructions: Stand tall, feet hip-width apart.
Raise your right arm overhead.
Lean slowly to the left, reaching long through your right side.
Keep both feet planted and hips square.
Hold 30 seconds each side. Perform 2–3 times.

name: Prone Quad Stretch
description: Deeper quad stretch than standing version — gravity assists the stretch for maximum release.
instructions: Lie face down on the floor.
Bend your right knee and reach back to hold your ankle.
Gently pull your heel toward your glute.
Keep your hips on the floor — don't let them rise.
Hold 30–45 seconds each leg.

name: Wall Hip Flexor Stretch
description: Uses a wall for support to get a deep hip flexor stretch safely — great for beginners.
instructions: Kneel facing away from a wall.
Place your right foot up on the wall behind you.
Step your left foot forward into a lunge.
Shift hips forward — feel the deep stretch in right hip.
Hold 45 seconds each side.
